exam.name: Victorian Selective Entry Examination
exam.subject: Verbal Reasoning
exam.description: Comprehensive study notes to master all verbal reasoning problem types for the Victorian Selective Entry Exam.
exam.usage_guide: Study each problem type until you can identify it instantly. Practise the strategy for each type, then do timed drills. Build vocabulary daily — it underpins almost every verbal reasoning task.
exam.golden_rule: Verbal reasoning tests how you think with language, not just what words you know. Read carefully, eliminate wrong answers systematically, and trust logic over gut feel.

core_skills_tested.description: Understanding word meanings, synonyms, antonyms, and nuance
Identifying relationships between pairs of words
Understanding, interpreting, and evaluating written passages
Identifying assumptions, evaluating arguments, detecting flaws

key_advice: Read every word carefully — a single word can change the entire answer
Eliminate clearly wrong answers first, then choose from what remains
Do not spend more than 45 seconds on any one question
Build vocabulary every day — read widely, look up unfamiliar words

categories.overview: Analogies test your ability to identify the relationship between a pair of words and apply that same relationship to another pair. These are among the most common and important verbal reasoning question types.

topics.title: Understanding Analogies
identification_cues: You see: 'A is to B as C is to ___'
You see colon notation: 'CAT : KITTEN :: DOG : ___'
Asked to find a word that completes the relationship

steps: Step 1: Identify the relationship between the first pair (A and B)
Step 2: Express it as a precise sentence: 'A is the ___ of B' or 'A [verb] B'
Step 3: Apply the exact same relationship to C to find D
Step 4: Check all options — eliminate any that don't fit the precise relationship

Be as SPECIFIC as possible when defining the relationship. 'Glove is to hand' — don't say 'one covers the other'; say 'a glove is worn on a hand'. The more specific your bridge sentence, the easier it is to eliminate wrong answers.

worked_example.question: AUTHOR : NOVEL :: COMPOSER : ___
steps: Step 1: Identify the relationship between the first pair (A and B) → An author creates a novel (their work). A composer creates a symphony (their work). Note: 'piano' is a tool, 'concert' is an event, 'musician' is a person — none fit 'creates a work'.
Step 2: Express it as a precise sentence: 'A is the ___ of B' or 'A [verb] B'
Step 3: Apply the exact same relationship to C to find D
Step 4: Check all options — eliminate any that don't fit the precise relationship → Verify: Symphony ✓

Be as SPECIFIC as possible when defining the relationship. 'Glove is to hand' — don't say 'one covers the other'; say 'a glove is worn on a hand'. The more specific your bridge sentence, the easier it is to eliminate wrong answers. → Remove obviously wrong options

analogy_relationship_types.description: The words have the same or similar meaning
analogy_relationship_types.bridge_sentence: Happy means the same as joyful
Hot is the opposite of cold

analogy_relationship_types.description: The first word is a part of the second
analogy_relationship_types.bridge_sentence: A petal is part of a flower

analogy_relationship_types.description: The first word contains the second
The first word is a type/member of the second
analogy_relationship_types.bridge_sentence: An oak is a type of tree

analogy_relationship_types.description: The first word is used to perform the action of the second
analogy_relationship_types.bridge_sentence: A pen is used to write

analogy_relationship_types.description: The first word uses the second as their tool
The first word creates/produces the second
The first word works in the second
analogy_relationship_types.bridge_sentence: A doctor works in a hospital

analogy_relationship_types.description: The first word is the adult and the second is its offspring
analogy_relationship_types.bridge_sentence: A kitten is the young of a cat

analogy_relationship_types.description: The first word makes the sound of the second
analogy_relationship_types.bridge_sentence: A dog makes the sound bark

analogy_relationship_types.description: The first word lives in the second
analogy_relationship_types.bridge_sentence: A bird lives in a nest

analogy_relationship_types.description: The first word's collective noun is the second
analogy_relationship_types.bridge_sentence: A group of fish is called a school

analogy_relationship_types.description: One word is a more/less extreme version of the other
analogy_relationship_types.bridge_sentence: Hot is a more extreme version of warm

analogy_relationship_types.description: The first word causes the second
The first word is made from the second
The first word is done to/with the second
The words share a grammatical relationship (e.g., noun/verb, singular/plural)
analogy_relationship_types.bridge_sentence: Ran is the past tense of run

analogy_relationship_types.description: The second word is a characteristic of the first

examples.question: AUTHOR : NOVEL :: COMPOSER : ___
solution_steps: An author creates a novel (their work). A composer creates a symphony (their work). Note: 'piano' is a tool, 'concert' is an event, 'musician' is a person — none fit 'creates a work'.

examples.question: MICROSCOPE : BIOLOGIST :: TELESCOPE : ___
solution_steps: A microscope is the tool used by a biologist. A telescope is the tool used by an astronomer.

examples.question: WHISPER : SHOUT :: TRICKLE : ___
solution_steps: Shout is a much more intense version of whisper. Pour is a much more intense version of trickle. (Flood is too extreme and is a noun.)

tips_and_tricks.tip: Form a precise 'bridge sentence' before looking at the options
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Create a specific sentence linking the pair: "A [relationship] B". Test each option with the same sentence.
tips_and_tricks.example: GLOVE : HAND → "A glove is worn on a hand" → SHOE : ? → "A shoe is worn on a foot" → FOOT ✓

tips_and_tricks.tip: If two options seem to fit, make your bridge sentence MORE specific to narrow it down
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Create a specific sentence linking the pair: "A [relationship] B". Test each option with the same sentence.
tips_and_tricks.example: GLOVE : HAND → "A glove is worn on a hand" → SHOE : ? → "A shoe is worn on a foot" → FOOT ✓

tips_and_tricks.tip: Watch the ORDER of the relationship — DOCTOR:HOSPITAL is not the same as HOSPITAL:DOCTOR
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a common mistake that costs marks in exams. Make sure you understand the difference.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Check that your answer fits the same PART OF SPEECH as the missing word
tips_and_tricks.explanation: A quick verification step catches careless errors and can save marks.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Eliminate any option that is clearly unrelated to the third word first
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Remove options you know are wrong to improve your odds. Even eliminating one option helps.
tips_and_tricks.example: 4 options, eliminate 2 wrong ones → 50% chance instead of 25% on remaining two

common_mistakes: Choosing a word that is related to C but doesn't match the A:B relationship
Ignoring part of speech (e.g., choosing a verb when a noun is needed)
Picking an associated word instead of the correctly related one
topics.concept_explanation: An analogy states that two words are related in the same way as another two words. The format is: A is to B as C is to D (written A : B :: C : D).

categories.overview: These questions test vocabulary depth — not just whether you know a word, but whether you know its precise meaning and how it relates to other words.

topics.title: Synonyms
identification_cues: Asked: 'Which word means the same as ___?'
Asked: 'Which word is closest in meaning to ___?'
A single word is given in capitals and you must find its match

steps: Step 1: Define the given word in your own words before looking at options
Step 2: Eliminate options you know are wrong
Step 3: For remaining options, test each in a sentence to see which fits best
Step 4: Watch for 'close but not quite' traps — similar words with different nuances
If you don't know a word, use word-part knowledge (prefix/root/suffix) to guess meaning

worked_example.question: BENEVOLENT most nearly means:
steps: Step 1: Define the given word in your own words before looking at options
Step 2: Eliminate options you know are wrong → Remove obviously wrong options
Step 3: For remaining options, test each in a sentence to see which fits best
Step 4: Watch for 'close but not quite' traps — similar words with different nuances
If you don't know a word, use word-part knowledge (prefix/root/suffix) to guess meaning
Answer: Generous

nuance_guide.description: Synonyms are rarely perfectly identical. The best synonym shares the CORE meaning AND the connotation (positive/negative/neutral tone).

examples.note: Choose the option that best preserves both meaning AND tone

examples.trap: Reckless (doing something dangerous but without good judgment)

examples.question: BENEVOLENT most nearly means:
examples.explanation: Benevolent means well-meaning and kind/generous. Strict is almost opposite. Timid and clever are unrelated.
examples.word_breakdown: bene (Latin: good/well) + volent (Latin: wishing) = well-wishing → generous/kind

examples.question: LETHARGIC most nearly means:
examples.explanation: Lethargic means lacking energy, sluggish, or drowsy. Energetic is the antonym.

vocabulary_strategies: Learn Latin and Greek roots — they unlock hundreds of words at once
Learn words in word families: BENEFIT, BENEVOLENT, BENEFACTOR, BENIGN
Use a new word in a sentence the same day you learn it
Read widely — novels, newspapers, quality non-fiction

tips_and_tricks.tip: The correct synonym often sounds 'elevated' — not too casual, not extreme
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: not too casual, not extreme

tips_and_tricks.tip: Be wary of words that look similar but mean something different (false friends)
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Some words look alike but have different meanings. Check carefully before choosing.
tips_and_tricks.example: "Accept" (receive) vs "except" (excluding). "Affect" (verb) vs "effect" (noun).

topics.concept_explanation: Find the word that means the same or most nearly the same as the given word.
common_mistakes: Choosing a word that is related but not similar in meaning (e.g., 'rain' for 'cloud')
Not considering the specific context — a word may have multiple meanings
Picking the first familiar-sounding word without checking all options
Confusing synonyms with antonyms under time pressure

topics.title: Antonyms
identification_cues: Asked: 'Which word is most opposite in meaning to ___?'
Asked: 'Which word means the opposite of ___?'

steps: Step 1: Define the given word clearly
Step 2: Think of the opposite concept in your own words
Step 3: Match to the best option
Step 4: Double-check — the trap is often a synonym, not an antonym
Prefixes un-, in-, im-, dis-, non- create antonyms: happy → unhappy, possible → impossible

worked_example.question: The antonym of OBSTINATE is:
steps: Step 1: Define the given word clearly
Step 2: Think of the opposite concept in your own words
Step 3: Match to the best option
Step 4: Double-check — the trap is often a synonym, not an antonym → Verify: Flexible ✓
Prefixes un-, in-, im-, dis-, non- create antonyms: happy → unhappy, possible → impossible

examples.question: The antonym of OBSTINATE is:
examples.explanation: Obstinate means stubbornly refusing to change — its opposite is flexible or open-minded. 'Stubborn' is actually a synonym (common trap!).
examples.trap_warning: Stubborn is the synonym, NOT the antonym — a classic trick.

examples.question: The antonym of LUCID is:
examples.explanation: Lucid means clear and easy to understand (or mentally clear). Its opposite is confused/unclear.

tips_and_tricks.tip: The SYNONYM of the word is often included as a trap — always check you haven't chosen it by mistake
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: always check you haven't chosen it by mistake

tips_and_tricks.tip: Think about the CONCEPT opposite, not just a word that sounds different
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

topics.concept_explanation: Find the word that means the opposite of the given word.
common_mistakes: Choosing a word that is merely different rather than truly opposite
Not considering the correct part of speech — the antonym should be the same word type
Confusing antonyms with synonyms when reading quickly
Overlooking that some words have different antonyms depending on context (e.g., 'light' can mean 'not heavy' or 'not dark')

categories.overview: These questions test your ability to group words by their properties, identify the odd one out, or find a hidden connection between words.

topics.title: Odd One Out
identification_cues: Asked: 'Which word does not belong?'
Asked: 'Which is the odd one out?'
A list of 4–5 words is given

steps: Step 1: Try to find what THREE or more words have in common
Step 2: The word that does NOT share that property is the odd one out
Step 3: Test multiple groupings — there may be a misleading surface connection

worked_example.question: Which word does NOT belong? APPLE, MANGO, CARROT, ORANGE, GRAPE
steps: Step 1: Try to find what THREE or more words have in common
Step 2: The word that does NOT share that property is the odd one out
Step 3: Test multiple groupings — there may be a misleading surface connection
Answer: Carrot

categories_to_consider: Category membership (e.g., all animals, but one is a plant)
Function (e.g., all writing tools, but one is a cutting tool)
Size or degree (e.g., all large, but one is small)
Grammatical form (e.g., all verbs, but one is a noun)

examples.question: Which word does NOT belong? APPLE, MANGO, CARROT, ORANGE, GRAPE
examples.explanation: Apple, Mango, Orange, and Grape are all fruits. Carrot is a vegetable.

examples.question: Which word does NOT belong? PIANO, VIOLIN, GUITAR, DRUM, FLUTE
examples.explanation: Piano, Violin, Guitar, and Flute all produce musical notes with definite pitch. A drum is a percussion instrument with indefinite pitch. Also: Piano, Violin, Guitar, Flute all have strings or create vibrations through air columns; drum uses a membrane.

examples.question: Which word does NOT belong? RECTANGLE, SQUARE, CIRCLE, TRIANGLE, PENTAGON
examples.explanation: Rectangle, Square, Triangle, and Pentagon are all polygons (straight-sided). A circle has no straight sides or corners.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Look beyond the obvious category — there may be a deeper classification
tips_and_tricks.explanation: For odd-one-out, find the category that connects the majority. The odd word breaks that pattern.
tips_and_tricks.example: Apple, Banana, Carrot, Mango → Carrot is odd (vegetable among fruits)

tips_and_tricks.tip: If two answers seem possible, the correct one has the CLEAREST reason for exclusion
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Think: what do 3–4 of the words share that the odd one out doesn't?
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

topics.concept_explanation: Four or five words are given — find the one that does NOT belong with the others.
common_mistakes: Identifying a superficial connection instead of the deeper shared category
Finding a reason why each word could be the odd one out — focus on the strongest connection
Not considering all possible grouping criteria (meaning, grammar, letter patterns, etc.)
Rushing and not checking that your chosen 'odd one' truly does not fit the others' shared trait

topics.title: Word Classification / Grouping
identification_cues: Asked: 'What do these words have in common?'
Asked: 'Which of the following belongs to the same group as ___?'

steps: Identify the precise rule that connects the group members
Test each option against that rule
Be alert to multiple possible rules — choose the most specific/precise one

worked_example.question: RUBY, EMERALD, SAPPHIRE, DIAMOND — which word completes this group? GOLD / AMETHYST / PEARL / SILVER
steps: Identify the precise rule that connects the group members
Test each option against that rule
Be alert to multiple possible rules — choose the most specific/precise one
Answer: Amethyst

examples.question: RUBY, EMERALD, SAPPHIRE, DIAMOND — which word completes this group? GOLD / AMETHYST / PEARL / SILVER
examples.explanation: Ruby, Emerald, Sapphire, and Diamond are all precious gemstones (minerals). Amethyst is also a gemstone. Gold, Silver, and Pearl are not gemstones.

examples.question: Which word does NOT belong: oak, elm, pine, daisy?
examples.explanation: Oak, elm, pine are all trees. Daisy is a flower.

examples.question: Which word does NOT belong: whisper, shout, murmur, sprint?
examples.explanation: Whisper, shout, murmur are all ways of speaking. Sprint is a way of running.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Narrow down the category as specifically as possible
tips_and_tricks.explanation: For odd-one-out, find the category that connects the majority. The odd word breaks that pattern.
tips_and_tricks.example: Apple, Banana, Carrot, Mango → Carrot is odd (vegetable among fruits)

tips_and_tricks.tip: Common grouping categories: animals, countries, capitals, planets, elements, sports, instruments
tips_and_tricks.explanation: For odd-one-out, find the category that connects the majority. The odd word breaks that pattern.
tips_and_tricks.example: Apple, Banana, Carrot, Mango → Carrot is odd (vegetable among fruits)

topics.concept_explanation: Words must be sorted into groups based on a shared property, or you must identify what property a set of words shares.
common_mistakes: Classifying based on surface similarity rather than the underlying shared feature
Not considering that words can belong to multiple categories — choose the best fit
Ignoring less obvious groupings like grammatical category or connotation
Not testing your grouping by checking that ALL members fit the rule

topics.title: Hidden Words
identification_cues: Asked: 'Find a hidden word in this sentence'
The hidden word is formed by letters at the end of one word and the beginning of the next

steps: Read across word boundaries, combining the end of one word with the start of the next
Slide a 3–5 letter window across the sentence, checking for real words
The hidden word usually spans exactly TWO consecutive words

worked_example.question: Find a hidden word: 'She swept he room clean'
steps: Read across word boundaries, combining the end of one word with the start of the next
Slide a 3–5 letter window across the sentence, checking for real words → Verify: THE ✓
The hidden word usually spans exactly TWO consecutive words

examples.question: Find a hidden word: 'She swept he room clean'
examples.explanation: swepT HE → the letters T-H-E are hidden spanning 'swept' and 'he'

examples.question: Find a hidden word: 'The carpet ends here'
examples.explanation: carpeT ENDs → T-E-N-D spans 'carpet' and 'ends'

examples.question: Find a hidden word: 'He scrambled eggs for dinner'
examples.explanation: scramBLED → wait, check spanning: scramblED EGGs = DEGG? No. scram-BLED spans within 'scrambled' → try: scramblED EGgs = DEGG. Re-check: scRAMbled = RAM. Answer: RAM is hidden in 'scrambled'.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Focus on word BOUNDARIES — the hidden word always spans at least two words
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: the hidden word always spans at least two words

tips_and_tricks.tip: Say the sentence aloud — you can sometimes hear the hidden word
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: you can sometimes hear the hidden word

tips_and_tricks.tip: The hidden word is typically 3–5 letters long
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

topics.concept_explanation: A word is hidden inside a phrase or sentence — you must find it.
common_mistakes: Looking only within individual words instead of across word boundaries
Missing the hidden word because of skipping over short common words like 'the', 'and'
Not reading systematically letter by letter through the phrase
Finding a shorter hidden word when a longer correct answer exists

categories.overview: A strong vocabulary is the foundation of all verbal reasoning. These topics cover word meanings, word parts, and how words are used in context.

topics.title: Word Meanings in Context
identification_cues: A word in bold or underlined within a sentence
Asked: 'In this context, the word ___ means...'

steps: Step 1: Read the whole sentence — never look at the word in isolation
Step 2: Use surrounding words as clues (context clues)
Step 3: Substitute your guess back in to check it makes sense
Step 4: For multi-meaning words, choose the meaning that fits THIS context

worked_example.question: In the sentence 'The coach tried to buoy the team's spirits before the final', the word BUOY most nearly means:
steps: Step 1: Read the whole sentence — never look at the word in isolation
Step 2: Use surrounding words as clues (context clues)
Step 3: Substitute your guess back in to check it makes sense → Plug in the values from the question
Step 4: For multi-meaning words, choose the meaning that fits THIS context
Answer: Lift

context_clue_types.description: The sentence defines the word directly
context_clue_types.example: 'The archaeologist, someone who studies ancient civilisations, found a remarkable artefact.'

context_clue_types.description: A nearby word has a similar meaning
context_clue_types.example: 'She was loquacious — her friend never got a word in because she talked so much.'

context_clue_types.description: A contrasting word gives the opposite meaning
context_clue_types.example: 'Unlike his gregarious sister, Tom was reclusive and rarely left the house.'
'Fauna such as kangaroos, wombats, and echidnas are native to Australia.'

context_clue_types.description: The overall tone and topic suggest the meaning
context_clue_types.example: 'The storm was ferocious — trees bent double, roofs flew off, and waves crashed over the harbour wall.'

examples.question: In the sentence 'The coach tried to buoy the team's spirits before the final', the word BUOY most nearly means:
examples.explanation: Here 'buoy' is used as a verb meaning to support or lift (as a buoy keeps something afloat). 'Float' is the noun/literal meaning — the trap.

examples.question: 'The bank issued a statement about the breach.' What does 'breach' mean here?
examples.explanation: In a banking context, 'breach' means a violation of security or rules (data breach).

examples.question: 'Her composure during the crisis was remarkable.' What does 'composure' mean?
examples.explanation: 'Composure' = the state of being calm and in control, especially under pressure.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Always read the WHOLE sentence — never define a word in isolation
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: never define a word in isolation

tips_and_tricks.tip: Multi-meaning words: the most common meaning is often the trap; look for the contextual meaning
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a frequently tested trap in exams. Being aware of it gives you an advantage.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Substitute your answer back into the sentence to verify
tips_and_tricks.explanation: A quick verification step catches careless errors and can save marks.

topics.concept_explanation: A word is used in a sentence or passage and you must determine its meaning from context, especially when it has multiple meanings.
common_mistakes: Choosing the most common meaning of the word without reading the context
Ignoring context clues in the surrounding sentence
Not substituting your chosen meaning back into the sentence to verify it makes sense
Confusing connotation (feeling/association) with denotation (dictionary meaning)

topics.title: Prefixes, Suffixes & Root Words
identification_cues: You encounter an unfamiliar word
You can see recognisable parts at the start or end

tips_and_tricks.tip: Break unknown words into prefix + root + suffix: un-predict-able = not + foresee + capable of
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Break unknown words into parts: prefix (before) + root (core meaning) + suffix (after).
tips_and_tricks.example: "un-break-able" → un (not) + break + able (can be) = cannot be broken

tips_and_tricks.tip: Knowing 30 key roots can help you decode 1000+ English words
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Break unknown words into parts: prefix (before) + root (core meaning) + suffix (after).
tips_and_tricks.example: "un-break-able" → un (not) + break + able (can be) = cannot be broken

tips_and_tricks.tip: Even a partial match helps: if you recognise 'bene' = good, 'malevolent' must be bad
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Practise word families: PREDICT, PREDICTION, PREDICTABLE, UNPREDICTABLE
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

examples.question: What does the prefix 'un-' mean in 'unhappy'?
examples.explanation: 'un-' means not or opposite of. Unhappy = not happy.

examples.question: The suffix '-ology' means the study of. What does 'biology' mean?
examples.explanation: 'bio' = life, '-ology' = study of. Biology = study of life.

examples.question: Which word uses the prefix 'mis-' correctly?
examples.explanation: 'mis-' means wrongly or badly. Misunderstand = understand wrongly.

examples.question: The root 'bene' means 'good'. Which word means 'a person who does good'?
examples.explanation: 'bene' (good) + 'factor' (doer) = one who does good, a donor.

examples.question: What does 'circumnavigate' mean, given 'circum-' = around and 'navigate' = to sail?
examples.explanation: Circumnavigate = to sail/travel completely around (e.g., circumnavigate the globe).

steps: Break unknown words into parts: prefix + root + suffix
Use context clues from surrounding text
Consider words with similar roots you already know
Learn common Latin and Greek roots for exam advantage

worked_example.question: What does the prefix 'un-' mean in 'unhappy'?
steps: Break unknown words into parts: prefix + root + suffix
Use context clues from surrounding text
Consider words with similar roots you already know
Learn common Latin and Greek roots for exam advantage
Answer: Not

topics.concept_explanation: Understanding word parts allows you to decode unknown words by breaking them into meaningful components.
common_mistakes: Assuming a prefix always means the same thing — context matters (e.g., 'in-' can mean 'not' or 'into')
Not recognising common Latin and Greek roots that appear in many English words
Confusing similar prefixes like 'pre-' (before) and 'pro-' (forward/for)
Incorrectly splitting words — not every word starting with 're' has 're-' as a prefix (e.g., 'real')

topics.title: Multiple Meaning Words (Homonyms & Polysemy)
identification_cues: A common, simple word used in an unexpected way
The sentence doesn't make sense with the most obvious meaning

tips_and_tricks.tip: Never assume the most common meaning — always use context
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: always use context

tips_and_tricks.tip: Substitute each meaning back into the sentence to test which works
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Pay attention to part of speech — is the word being used as a noun, verb, or adjective?
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: is the word being used as a noun, verb, or adjective?

examples.question: Which meaning of 'bank' fits: 'She sat on the bank of the river'?
examples.explanation: 'Bank' has multiple meanings. In this context, it means the raised edge alongside a river.

examples.question: 'The match was a draw.' What does 'draw' mean here?
examples.explanation: In sport context, 'draw' means neither side won — a tie.

examples.question: 'He tried to console his friend after the console broke.' How many meanings of 'console' are used?
examples.explanation: First 'console' (verb) = to comfort. Second 'console' (noun) = a gaming/electronic device.

steps: Break unknown words into parts: prefix + root + suffix
Use context clues from surrounding text
Consider words with similar roots you already know
Learn common Latin and Greek roots for exam advantage

worked_example.question: Which meaning of 'bank' fits: 'She sat on the bank of the river'?
steps: Break unknown words into parts: prefix + root + suffix
Use context clues from surrounding text
Consider words with similar roots you already know
Learn common Latin and Greek roots for exam advantage
Answer: Edge of a river

topics.concept_explanation: Words that have more than one meaning — you must identify which meaning is intended.
common_mistakes: Defaulting to the most familiar meaning without considering the context
Not recognising that the same word can function as different parts of speech (e.g., 'light' as noun, adjective, verb)
Confusing homonyms (same spelling) with homophones (same sound, different spelling)
Overlooking less common meanings that fit the context perfectly

topics.title: Homophones & Commonly Confused Words

examples.question: Choose the correct word: '___ going to be late if they don't hurry.'
examples.explanation: 'They're' = they are. 'Their' = belonging to them. 'There' = a place.

examples.question: Choose correctly: 'The new rule will ___ everyone in the school.'
examples.explanation: 'Affect' is usually a verb (to influence). 'Effect' is usually a noun (the result). Here we need the verb.

examples.question: 'The ___ of the school announced a new ___ about phone use.' Fill both blanks:
examples.explanation: 'Principal' = head of school (person). 'Principle' = a rule or belief.

steps: Break unknown words into parts: prefix + root + suffix
Use context clues from surrounding text
Consider words with similar roots you already know
Learn common Latin and Greek roots for exam advantage

worked_example.question: Choose the correct word: '___ going to be late if they don't hurry.'
steps: Break unknown words into parts: prefix + root + suffix
Use context clues from surrounding text
Consider words with similar roots you already know
Learn common Latin and Greek roots for exam advantage
Answer: They're

tips_and_tricks.tip: Read all options before choosing your answer
tips_and_tricks.explanation: The first plausible answer may not be the best one — always consider all choices.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Use elimination to narrow down options
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Removing obviously wrong answers improves your chances, even if you have to guess.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Look for context clues in the surrounding words
tips_and_tricks.explanation: The words around a blank or target word often hint at the answer.

topics.concept_explanation: Words that sound the same but have different meanings and spellings. A frequent source of errors and exam questions.
identification_cues: Question asks you to choose the correct word from similar-sounding options
Involves completing a sentence with the right form of a homophone pair
Tests knowledge of commonly confused word pairs
common_mistakes: Confusing 'their' (belonging to them), 'there' (place), and 'they're' (they are)
Mixing up 'affect' (verb, to influence) and 'effect' (noun, result)
Using 'your' instead of 'you're' or 'its' instead of 'it's'
Not using a mnemonic or memory trick to remember the difference

categories.overview: These questions test your ability to choose the word(s) that best complete a sentence — requiring both vocabulary knowledge and understanding of sentence logic.

topics.title: Single-Blank Sentence Completion
identification_cues: A sentence with a blank (___ or ___)
Asked: 'Which word best completes this sentence?'

steps: Step 1: Read the whole sentence and predict what type of word is missing
Step 2: Think of your own word to fill the gap before reading options
Step 3: Match your word to the closest option
Step 4: Plug each option in and read the sentence — eliminate any that sound wrong
Step 5: Check grammar — does the word fit (noun/verb/adjective/adverb)?

worked_example.question: Although the scientist had worked on the problem for years, the solution remained ___.
steps: Step 1: Read the whole sentence and predict what type of word is missing
Step 2: Think of your own word to fill the gap before reading options
Step 3: Match your word to the closest option
Step 4: Plug each option in and read the sentence — eliminate any that sound wrong → Plug in the values from the question
Step 5: Check grammar — does the word fit (noun/verb/adjective/adverb)? → Verify: elusive ✓

signal_words.meaning: Contrast — the blank word is opposite in tone to what came before

signal_words.signal: Therefore / So / Thus / As a result
signal_words.meaning: Consequence — the blank word logically follows from what came before
Addition — the blank word is similar in tone to what came before
The blank is a specific instance of a broader category mentioned

examples.question: Although the scientist had worked on the problem for years, the solution remained ___.
examples.explanation: 'Although' signals contrast. She worked hard, so you'd expect progress — but 'although' means the opposite happened. The solution was hard to find = elusive.

examples.question: The new law was ___, affecting every citizen in the country.
examples.explanation: 'Every citizen' signals something widespread and comprehensive = sweeping. Trivial means unimportant (opposite). Personal and brief don't fit 'every citizen'.

examples.question: The old house was so ___ that nobody dared enter it at night.
examples.explanation: 'Nobody dared enter at night' implies something scary or unsettling — 'eerie' means strange and frightening.

examples.question: Despite his ___ appearance, the man was actually very kind and generous.
examples.explanation: 'Despite' signals contrast — his appearance suggests one thing (scary/tough), but reality is different (kind).

tips_and_tricks.tip: PREDICT your own word before looking at the options — this prevents options from misleading you
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Getting this step right early prevents cascading errors through the rest of the solution.
tips_and_tricks.example: this prevents options from misleading you

tips_and_tricks.tip: Signal words (however, therefore, because) tell you what logical relationship to expect
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Read the completed sentence aloud — wrong answers often 'sound' off
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: wrong answers often 'sound' off

tips_and_tricks.tip: Check all options, even if the first seems right
tips_and_tricks.explanation: The first plausible answer may not be the best. Read all options to find the closest match.
tips_and_tricks.example: Question: synonym for "big". Options: large, huge, vast, grand → "large" is closest (same intensity)

topics.concept_explanation: One word is missing from a sentence. Choose the word that best completes it in meaning and grammar.
common_mistakes: Not using signal words (but, although, therefore) to determine whether the blank needs a similar or contrasting word
Choosing a word that sounds right without checking it makes grammatical sense
Ignoring the overall tone of the sentence when selecting the answer
Not reading the entire sentence before choosing — the clue may come after the blank

topics.title: Double-Blank Sentence Completion

steps: Step 1: Identify the relationship between the two blanks (same direction / opposite / cause-effect)
Step 2: Eliminate any option pair where EITHER word doesn't fit
Step 3: The correct pair must work together — both words must fit the tone and logic

worked_example.question: The explorer was ___ by the hostile conditions but remained ___ in her determination.
steps: Step 1: Identify the relationship between the two blanks (same direction / opposite / cause-effect)
Step 2: Eliminate any option pair where EITHER word doesn't fit → Remove obviously wrong options
Step 3: The correct pair must work together — both words must fit the tone and logic
Answer: B: daunted / unwavering

examples.question: The explorer was ___ by the hostile conditions but remained ___ in her determination.
examples.explanation: 'But' signals contrast. First blank: negative (conditions were hostile = she was daunted). Second blank: positive/strong (contrasting with being daunted = unwavering).

examples.question: The scientist's theory was initially ___, but after years of ___, it was widely accepted.
examples.explanation: 'Initially... but after years... widely accepted' = progression from negative to positive. Rejected → research → accepted.

examples.question: The ___ storm forced the sailors to seek ___ in a nearby harbour.
examples.explanation: A storm that 'forces' action must be severe (fierce). Seeking safety = shelter.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Eliminate one blank at a time — if the first word is wrong, the whole pair is wrong
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Remove options you know are wrong to improve your odds. Even eliminating one option helps.
tips_and_tricks.example: 4 options, eliminate 2 wrong ones → 50% chance instead of 25% on remaining two

tips_and_tricks.tip: Look for the relationship signal word to determine if blanks go in the same or opposite direction
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Tone must be consistent — don't mix a formal word with an informal one
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: don't mix a formal word with an informal one

topics.concept_explanation: Two words are missing from one sentence. Both must fit grammatically and logically.
identification_cues: Sentence has two blanks to fill simultaneously
Answer options are presented as pairs of words
The relationship between the two blanks is often contrasting, cause-effect, or parallel
common_mistakes: Choosing a pair where one word fits but the other doesn't
Not considering how the two blanks relate to each other
Ignoring transitional words that connect the two parts of the sentence
Only checking the first blank and assuming the second word must also be correct

topics.title: Cloze Passages

steps: Read the entire passage FIRST before attempting any blanks
Identify the topic and tone of the passage
Fill blanks you're confident about first
Use filled blanks as context clues for remaining gaps
Ensure the passage makes logical sense as a whole

worked_example.question: Fill in the blanks: 'The ancient castle stood on a ___ overlooking the sea. Its ___ walls had withstood centuries of storms.'
steps: Read the entire passage FIRST before attempting any blanks
Identify the topic and tone of the passage
Fill blanks you're confident about first
Use filled blanks as context clues for remaining gaps
Ensure the passage makes logical sense as a whole
Answer: cliff / towering

tips_and_tricks.tip: Coherence matters — don't pick words in isolation; think about how they flow
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: don't pick words in isolation; think about how they flow

tips_and_tricks.tip: Repetition of key words/ideas from the passage often signals what the blank should be
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Check that tense and grammar are consistent throughout
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Your answer must match grammatically: same tense, same number (singular/plural), same part of speech.
tips_and_tricks.example: "She ___ to school daily" → needs present tense verb → "walks" ✓, "walked" ✗ (wrong tense)

examples.question: Fill in the blanks: 'The ancient castle stood on a ___ overlooking the sea. Its ___ walls had withstood centuries of storms.'
examples.explanation: A castle overlooks the sea from a cliff (not mountain). Walls that withstand storms are towering (strong and tall).

examples.question: 'Despite the ___ weather, the explorers pressed on, their ___ unbroken.' Choose the best pair:
examples.explanation: 'Despite' signals contrast — bad weather but positive spirit. 'Harsh weather' + 'determination unbroken' creates logical contrast.

examples.question: 'The scientist's ___ discovery was initially met with ___, but eventually transformed the field.'
examples.explanation: 'Initially met with... but eventually transformed' = resistance first, then acceptance. Groundbreaking discoveries are often met with scepticism.

topics.concept_explanation: A short passage with multiple blanks — choose words to fill each gap, maintaining overall coherence.
identification_cues: A passage of text has multiple blanks (usually 5-10) throughout
Requires understanding the whole passage to fill each gap
Tests both vocabulary and reading comprehension simultaneously
common_mistakes: Filling blanks in isolation without reading the whole passage first
Not checking that your chosen word fits grammatically (correct tense, number, etc.)
Using a word that makes local sense but contradicts information elsewhere in the passage
Not returning to recheck all blanks after completing the passage

categories.overview: Reading comprehension tests your ability to understand, analyse, and interpret written texts. It requires active reading — not passive scanning.

topics.title: Comprehension Question Types

question_types.description: The answer is directly stated in the text
question_types.how_to_identify: Questions using 'According to the text...', 'The author states...'
question_types.strategy: Locate the relevant section, read carefully, match to the option
question_types.trap: Choosing an answer that is TRUE in general but not stated in THIS passage

question_types.description: The answer is implied but not directly stated — you must 'read between the lines'
question_types.how_to_identify: Questions using 'We can infer...', 'It is most likely that...', 'The passage suggests...'
question_types.strategy: Find clues in the text, reason logically from them to a conclusion
question_types.trap: Choosing an option that goes beyond what the text supports (over-inference)

question_types.description: Find the meaning of a word as used in the passage
question_types.how_to_identify: Questions using 'In line X, the word ___ means...'
question_types.strategy: Read the surrounding sentences, use context clues, eliminate unlikely options

question_types.description: Identify what the whole passage is mainly about or why it was written
question_types.how_to_identify: Questions using 'The main purpose of this passage is...', 'This text is mainly about...'
question_types.strategy: Look at the first and last paragraphs, identify the central argument or theme

question_types.description: Identify how the author feels about the subject
question_types.how_to_identify: Questions using 'The author's tone is...', 'The author seems to...'
question_types.strategy: Look for emotive language, positive/negative words, hedging or certainty

question_types.description: How is the text structured? What is the purpose of a specific paragraph?
question_types.how_to_identify: Questions about 'the role of paragraph X', 'why does the author begin with...'
question_types.strategy: Identify structural patterns: problem-solution, cause-effect, compare-contrast, narrative, argument

question_types.description: Which detail from the passage best supports a given statement?
question_types.how_to_identify: Questions using 'Which detail supports the claim that...'
question_types.strategy: Go back to the text, find the most directly relevant evidence

active_reading_steps: Step 1: Read the questions FIRST so you know what to look for
Step 2: Read the passage actively — underline key ideas, circle important words
Step 3: Answer literal questions directly from the text
Step 4: For inferential questions, reason from evidence in the text
Step 5: Eliminate options that are clearly wrong or only partially correct

tips_and_tricks.tip: The correct answer is almost always supported by SPECIFIC words in the text — go back and find them
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: go back and find them

tips_and_tricks.tip: Wrong answer types to watch for: too broad, too narrow, opposite of what's stated, not in the text, partially correct
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a frequently tested trap in exams. Being aware of it gives you an advantage.

tips_and_tricks.tip: For 'main idea' questions: avoid answers that are only about ONE part of the passage
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: The author's opinion ≠ universal fact — distinguish between the two
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a common mistake that costs marks in exams. Make sure you understand the difference.

examples.question: Read: 'The rainforest canopy blocks most sunlight from reaching the forest floor, creating a dark, humid environment where only specially adapted plants can survive.' What is the main idea?
options: The canopy affects life on the forest floor
examples.correct_answer: The canopy affects life on the forest floor
examples.explanation: The main idea connects the canopy (cause) to the conditions below (effect) and what survives there.
examples.answer: The canopy affects life on the forest floor

examples.question: Read: 'Although the invention was praised by critics, sales remained disappointingly low throughout the first year.' What does 'although' signal?
examples.explanation: 'Although' signals that what follows contradicts what came before — praise vs low sales.

examples.question: Read: 'Mrs Henderson slammed the door, threw her keys on the table, and sank into the armchair with a heavy sigh.' What can you infer about Mrs Henderson's mood?
examples.explanation: Slamming, throwing, and sighing heavily all indicate frustration or exhaustion — inference from actions, not stated directly.

steps: Read all options before choosing
Eliminate obviously wrong answers to improve your odds
Use context and word parts to decode unfamiliar words
If stuck between two options, re-read with each inserted to test fit

worked_example.question: Read: 'The rainforest canopy blocks most sunlight from reaching the forest floor, creating a dark, humid environment where only specially adapted plants can survive.' What is the main idea?
steps: Read all options before choosing
Eliminate obviously wrong answers to improve your odds → Remove obviously wrong options
Use context and word parts to decode unfamiliar words
If stuck between two options, re-read with each inserted to test fit
Answer: The canopy affects life on the forest floor
worked_example.answer: The canopy affects life on the forest floor

topics.concept_explanation: Different types of comprehension questions require different reading strategies.
identification_cues: A short passage is followed by questions about its content
Questions ask 'according to the passage', 'the author suggests', or 'it can be inferred'
Tests multiple skills: finding facts, making inferences, and understanding vocabulary
common_mistakes: Answering based on general knowledge rather than what the passage actually says
Not referring back to the passage to verify your answer
Confusing stated information with inferences
Spending too long reading the passage — skim first, then read questions, then re-read targeted sections

topics.title: Inference & Critical Reading
topics.what_inference_is_not: Inference is NOT making things up. It is logical reasoning from clues IN the text.
inference_framework: CLUE in text + BACKGROUND KNOWLEDGE = INFERENCE
The inference must be SUPPORTED by the text
The inference must not CONTRADICT anything in the text
The inference should be the MOST LOGICAL conclusion, not just possible

examples.passage_extract: Maria glanced at the darkening sky and quickened her pace. She had left her umbrella at home again.
examples.question: We can infer that Maria is concerned about:
examples.explanation: The darkening sky (storm clue) + forgotten umbrella (no protection) = logical inference she is worried about getting rained on. Nothing mentions work or losing the umbrella.

examples.question: Read: 'Jake glanced at his watch for the third time, tapping his foot and scanning the empty platform.' What can you infer?
options: Jake is waiting for a late train
examples.correct_answer: Jake is waiting for a late train
examples.explanation: Repeated watch-checking, foot-tapping (impatience), and empty platform suggest waiting for something that should have arrived.
examples.answer: Jake is waiting for a late train

examples.question: Read: 'The streetlights flickered on as Maria hurried home, her shadow stretching long behind her.' What time of day is it?
examples.explanation: Streetlights turning on = dusk/evening. Long shadows = low sun angle. She's hurrying = wants to get home before dark.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Ask: 'Does the text SHOW this, even if it doesn't SAY it?'
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Correct inferences are modest — they don't leap too far from the evidence
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: they don't leap too far from the evidence

tips_and_tricks.tip: Eliminate options that require information NOT found in or implied by the text
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a common mistake that costs marks in exams. Make sure you understand the difference.

steps: Read all options before choosing
Eliminate obviously wrong answers to improve your odds
Use context and word parts to decode unfamiliar words
If stuck between two options, re-read with each inserted to test fit

worked_example.question: We can infer that Maria is concerned about:
steps: Read all options before choosing
Eliminate obviously wrong answers to improve your odds → Remove obviously wrong options
Use context and word parts to decode unfamiliar words
If stuck between two options, re-read with each inserted to test fit
Answer: Getting wet in the rain

topics.concept_explanation: Drawing conclusions that go beyond what is literally stated, while staying within what the text supports.
identification_cues: Question uses words like 'infer', 'imply', 'suggest', or 'most likely'
Asks what can be concluded from the passage
Requires reading between the lines rather than finding stated facts
common_mistakes: Making assumptions that go beyond what the text supports
Confusing inference (supported by text evidence) with personal opinion
Choosing an answer that is true in real life but not supported by the specific passage
Not identifying the specific words or phrases in the text that support the inference

categories.overview: These questions use logical structures expressed in words — you must reason accurately from given statements to valid conclusions.

topics.title: Syllogisms & Deductive Reasoning
identification_cues: Two statements followed by: 'Therefore...' or 'Which conclusion must be true?'
All A are B, All B are C → therefore...?

steps: Step 1: Accept all premises as true — do not question them
Step 2: Find the logical chain: what does the combination of statements imply?
Step 3: The conclusion MUST follow — if it only MIGHT be true, it's not valid
Step 4: Use diagrams (circles within circles) to visualise the relationships

worked_example.question: All reptiles are cold-blooded. All cold-blooded animals are ectotherms. Which conclusion MUST be true?
steps: Step 1: Accept all premises as true — do not question them
Step 2: Find the logical chain: what does the combination of statements imply?
Step 3: The conclusion MUST follow — if it only MIGHT be true, it's not valid
Step 4: Use diagrams (circles within circles) to visualise the relationships
Answer: All reptiles are ectotherms

syllogism_patterns.pattern: All A are B. All B are C. → All A are C.
syllogism_patterns.example: All dogs are mammals. All mammals are warm-blooded. → All dogs are warm-blooded. ✓

syllogism_patterns.pattern: All A are B. X is an A. → X is a B.
syllogism_patterns.example: All planets orbit a star. Earth is a planet. → Earth orbits a star. ✓

syllogism_patterns.pattern: No A are B. X is an A. → X is not a B.
syllogism_patterns.example: No fish are mammals. A salmon is a fish. → A salmon is not a mammal. ✓

syllogism_patterns.pattern: Some A are B. (No guaranteed conclusion about all A or all B)
syllogism_patterns.example: Some athletes are doctors. Maria is an athlete. → We CANNOT conclude Maria is a doctor.

examples.question: All reptiles are cold-blooded. All cold-blooded animals are ectotherms. Which conclusion MUST be true?
examples.explanation: Chain: reptiles → cold-blooded → ectotherms. Therefore reptiles → ectotherms. Option A reverses the chain incorrectly — not all ectotherms need to be reptiles.

examples.question: All roses are flowers. All flowers need water. Therefore:
examples.explanation: If A→B and B→C, then A→C. Roses→flowers→need water, so roses need water.

examples.question: Some dogs are friendly. All friendly animals are popular. Therefore:
examples.explanation: 'Some dogs are friendly' + 'All friendly → popular' = those some dogs are popular. Not ALL dogs.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Draw Venn diagrams (circles) — it's the fastest way to see syllogism relationships
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This shortcut saves valuable time in timed exams while maintaining accuracy.
tips_and_tricks.example: it's the fastest way to see syllogism relationships

tips_and_tricks.tip: The conclusion must be NECESSARILY true, not just possibly true
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Never add information from outside the premises — only use what's given
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: only use what's given

tips_and_tricks.tip: 'Some' is your enemy — it rarely leads to a definite conclusion
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: it rarely leads to a definite conclusion

topics.concept_explanation: Two or more statements (premises) are given — you must determine what logically follows.

common_mistakes.description: 'All A are B' does NOT mean 'All B are A'
common_mistakes.example: All dogs are animals ≠ All animals are dogs

common_mistakes.description: 'If A then B. B is true.' does NOT mean 'A is true'
common_mistakes.example: If it rains, the ground is wet. The ground is wet. → Does NOT mean it rained (could be sprinklers).

common_mistakes.description: 'Some A are B' does NOT mean 'All A are B'
common_mistakes.example: Some birds can't fly ≠ All birds can't fly

topics.title: Coding & Decoding
identification_cues: A word is shown with its 'code' equivalent
You are asked to encode a new word or decode a code

common_coding_types.description: Each letter is moved forward or backward by a fixed number in the alphabet
common_coding_types.example: CAT → DBU (each letter +1): C→D, A→B, T→U

common_coding_types.description: The letters of the word are written backwards
Each letter is replaced by a symbol, number, or another letter according to a key
common_coding_types.example: If A=1, B=2, C=3... then CAT = 3-1-20

common_coding_types.description: Letters of the word are rearranged in a fixed pattern

steps: Compare the original word and its code letter by letter
Find the transformation rule (shift amount, reversal, substitution)
Verify the rule with any additional examples given
Apply the exact same rule to the new word

worked_example.question: If HOUSE is coded as IPVTF, what is the code for MOUSE?
steps: Compare the original word and its code letter by letter → H→I (+1), O→P (+1), U→V (+1), S→T (+1), E→F (+1)
Find the transformation rule (shift amount, reversal, substitution) → Rule: each letter shifts forward by 1 in the alphabet
Verify the rule with any additional examples given → MOUSE: M→N, O→P, U→V, S→T, E→F
Apply the exact same rule to the new word
Answer: NPVTF

examples.question: If HOUSE is coded as IPVTF, what is the code for MOUSE?
solution_steps: Rule: each letter shifts forward by 1 in the alphabet
MOUSE: M→N, O→P, U→V, S→T, E→F

examples.question: If CAT = 3-1-20 (A=1, B=2...), what is DOG?
examples.explanation: D=4, O=15, G=7. Each letter maps to its position in the alphabet.

examples.question: In a code, FISH = GJTI (each letter +1). What is BIRD?
examples.explanation: Each letter shifts +1: B→C, I→J, R→S, D→E. BIRD = CJSE.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Write the alphabet with numbers (A=1 to Z=26) for quick reference
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This shortcut saves valuable time in timed exams while maintaining accuracy.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Check your rule on ALL given examples before applying it
tips_and_tricks.explanation: A quick verification step catches careless errors and can save marks.

tips_and_tricks.tip: For letter shifts: if Z+1, it wraps around to A
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

topics.concept_explanation: Words are encoded into a pattern — you must identify the rule and apply it.
common_mistakes: Assuming simple one-step coding when the pattern involves multiple operations
Not checking the code against ALL given examples — it must work for every one
Confusing forward and backward letter shifts
Overlooking that the code may reverse the word or rearrange letters

topics.title: Statement & Conclusion Problems
identification_cues: A fact or scenario is stated
Two or more conclusions are given
Asked: 'Which conclusion(s) follow from the statement?'

steps: Read the statement carefully — accept it as 100% true
For each conclusion, ask: 'Does this NECESSARILY follow from the statement?'
A conclusion is valid only if it must be true given the statement
Do not use outside knowledge — only reason from what is given

worked_example.question: "All birds have feathers. A penguin is a bird." Is "A penguin has feathers" a valid conclusion?
steps: Identify premises: (1) All birds have feathers (2) A penguin is a bird
Apply deductive logic: penguin ∈ birds → penguin has feathers
Check: does conclusion follow necessarily? Yes — if ALL birds have feathers and penguin IS a bird, it MUST have feathers
Verify: no hidden assumptions or exceptions in the premises
worked_example.answer: Yes, valid conclusion (follows logically from both premises)

examples.statement: Most students who study daily for at least 30 minutes improve their grades.
conclusions: A: All students who study daily improve their grades
B: Studying daily for 30+ minutes is likely to improve grades
C: Students who don't study daily will not improve
examples.explanation: A is wrong — 'most' ≠ 'all'. B correctly reflects 'most' with 'likely'. C is wrong — it draws a conclusion about those not mentioned.

examples.question: Statement: 'All students who study hard pass the exam.' Conclusion: 'Raj passed the exam, so he studied hard.' Is this valid?
examples.explanation: The original says study hard → pass. But pass doesn't necessarily → studied hard (could be naturally gifted, lucky, etc.). This is the converse fallacy.

tips_and_tricks.tip: 'Most' does not mean 'all' — be precise about quantifiers
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: be precise about quantifiers

tips_and_tricks.tip: A valid conclusion must follow from the statement ALONE
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Absolute words like 'always', 'never', 'all', 'none' are usually traps in conclusions
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.

topics.concept_explanation: A statement is given — you must decide which conclusions logically follow.
common_mistakes: Using real-world knowledge to justify a conclusion instead of relying only on the given statements
Confusing 'possible' with 'definite' — a valid conclusion MUST follow, not just could follow
Not considering that multiple conclusions might follow from the same statements
Accepting a conclusion that seems reasonable but isn't directly supported by the premises

categories.overview: These questions test your understanding of how English sentences are constructed and whether language is used correctly.

topics.title: Parts of Speech

parts_of_speech.definition: A person, place, thing, or idea
An action or state of being
Describes a verb, adjective, or another adverb
Shows relationship between noun/pronoun and another word

types: Coordinating: for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so (FANBOYS)
Subordinating: because, although, when, if, since, unless
Correlating: either...or, neither...nor, not only...but also

examples.question: In 'The clever fox quickly jumped over the lazy dog', which word is an adverb?

topics.title: Common Grammar Rules

rules.description: The verb must agree in number with the subject
rules.incorrect: The team are playing well. (in formal Australian English, collective nouns take singular verbs)
rules.tricky_case: Phrases between subject and verb can confuse: 'The box of chocolates IS on the table' (subject = box, not chocolates)

rules.description: A pronoun must agree in number and gender with the noun it refers to
rules.correct: Each student must bring their own pencil.
rules.tricky_case: Everyone, someone, anyone take singular verbs but often plural pronouns in modern usage

rules.description: Used for possession or contractions — NOT for plurals
examples: Possession: the dog's bone (singular), the dogs' bones (plural)
Contraction: it's = it is; don't = do not
Trap: its (possession) has NO apostrophe: 'The cat licked its paw.'

confusables.rule: Affect is usually a verb, effect is usually a noun: 'Rain affects the game; the effect is a delay.'
their = belonging to them; there = a place; they're = they are
your = belonging to you; you're = you are
its = belonging to it; it's = it is
who = subject; whom = object: 'Who called?' / 'To whom did you speak?'
fewer = countable things (fewer apples); less = uncountable (less water)
lay takes an object (lay the book down); lie does not (lie down)

types.description: Two independent clauses joined by a conjunction

topics.title: Spelling Rules

rules.rule: i before e, except after c
Drop the 'e' before a suffix starting with a vowel
rules.exception: Keep 'e' before -able if needed for pronunciation: notice → noticeable

rules.rule: Double the final consonant before -ing / -ed when the word has one vowel before one final consonant (CVC pattern) and the stress is on the final syllable
Change y to i before a suffix (except -ing)

subrules: Words ending in -s, -sh, -ch, -x, -z: add -es (bus → buses, watch → watches)
Words ending in consonant + y: change y to i and add -es (city → cities)
Words ending in -f or -fe: often change to -ves (leaf → leaves, knife → knives)
Irregular plurals: child → children, mouse → mice, foot → feet, tooth → teeth

tips_and_tricks.tip: Say the word syllable by syllable when spelling it: ne-ces-sa-ry

animal_reference.description: Frequently tested in analogies and classification questions

before_exam_checklist: Revise all analogy relationship types — practise forming bridge sentences
Learn 5–10 new vocabulary words every week from the master list
Read a quality book, newspaper, or magazine article daily
Do timed verbal reasoning practice papers under exam conditions
Review all prefix, suffix, and root word lists until automatic
Practise spotting context clues in reading passages

time_management: Answer questions you're confident about first
Spend no more than 45 seconds per question before moving on
Mark questions you're unsure about and return if time allows
Never leave a multiple choice question blank — make your best guess
reading_strategy: For passages: skim-read first, then answer questions with targeted re-reading
Underline or mentally note key words in both question and passage
For analogy questions: form your bridge sentence BEFORE looking at options
elimination_strategy: Eliminate options you're certain are wrong
If two options remain, compare them directly — look for the subtle difference
Trust your process over your instinct if the two conflict

common_traps.trap: Choosing a synonym instead of an antonym
common_traps.fix: Re-read the question: does it ask for same meaning or opposite meaning?

common_traps.trap: Picking an 'associated' word in analogies instead of the 'related' word
common_traps.example: AUTHOR : NOVEL :: COMPOSER : Piano (associated) instead of Symphony (product)
common_traps.fix: Always apply the EXACT relationship from A:B to find D

common_traps.example: Concluding the character is 'devastated' when the text only says she 'frowned'
common_traps.fix: Stay close to what the text actually says — only infer what is strongly implied

common_traps.example: Thinking 'let the cat out of the bag' involves an actual cat
common_traps.fix: Ask: does the literal meaning make sense in context? If not, it's an idiom

common_traps.trap: Treating 'all' and 'some' as interchangeable in logic
common_traps.example: Some athletes are fast → All athletes are fast (WRONG)
common_traps.fix: Be precise with quantifiers: all, some, none, most, a few all mean different things

common_traps.example: All dogs are animals → All animals are dogs (WRONG)
common_traps.fix: The relationship only works one way unless stated otherwise

common_traps.trap: Using the most common word meaning when context implies another
common_traps.example: 'Buoy' chosen as a float (noun) when used as a verb meaning 'to lift'
common_traps.fix: Always determine part of speech and use context to find the intended meaning

comprehension_question_type_strategies.literal: Find it directly in the text
comprehension_question_type_strategies.inferential: Reason from clues in the text
comprehension_question_type_strategies.main_idea: Look at first and last paragraphs
comprehension_question_type_strategies.author_tone: Look for emotive and evaluative language
